subject: Chemistry
q: The mass of sodium acetate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COONa</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> required to prepare <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>35</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous solution is _____ g.<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close=""><mrow></mrow></mfenced></math> Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COONa</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>82</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) Round off to the nearest integer.
question: The mass of sodium acetate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COONa</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> required to prepare <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>35</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous solution is _____ g.<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close=""><mrow></mrow></mfenced></math> Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COONa</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>82</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) Round off to the nearest integer.
solution:
Given, Molarity= 0.35 M

Moles = Molarity × Volume in litres

=0.35×0.25

No. of moles = Mass/ Molar mass 

Mass = moles ×molar mass

=0.35×0.25×82.02=7.18 g

Ans. 7

subject: Chemistry
q: The osmotic pressure of a solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn></math> atm and that of a glucose solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn></math> atm. The osmotic pressure of a solution formed by mixing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of the sodium chloride solution with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of the glucose solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> atm. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is ________ (nearest integer)
question: The osmotic pressure of a solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn></math> atm and that of a glucose solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn></math> atm. The osmotic pressure of a solution formed by mixing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of the sodium chloride solution with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of the glucose solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> atm. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is ________ (nearest integer)
solution:
π=iCRT

On mixing two solution πfinal=π1V1+π2V2V1+V2

πfinal=0.1×1+0.2×23

            =0.1+0.43=0.53=5003×100-3atm

So,      X=167

subject: Chemistry
q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>363</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the vapour pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kPa</mi></math> and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mi>kPa</mi></math>. One mole of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are mixed. Assuming that this solution is ideal, the vapour pressure of the mixture is___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kPa</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>Round of to the Nearest Integer<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>)</mo></math>.
question: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>363</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the vapour pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kPa</mi></math> and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mi>kPa</mi></math>. One mole of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are mixed. Assuming that this solution is ideal, the vapour pressure of the mixture is___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kPa</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>Round of to the Nearest Integer<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>)</mo></math>.
solution:
Given PA0=21 kPa  ⇒PB0=18 kPa

→ An Ideal solution is prepared by mixing 1 mole A and 2 mole B.

→XA=13 and XB=23

→ According to Raoult's law

PT=XAPA0+XBPB0

⇒  PT=13×21+23×18

⇒  PT=7+12=19 kPa

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following liquid pairs shows a positive deviation from Raoult's law?
question: Which of the following liquid pairs shows a positive deviation from Raoult's law?
solution: Benzene in methanol breaks the $\mathrm{H}$ - bonding of the alcohol making its boiling point decrease \& there by its vapour pressure increases leading two +ve deviation.

subject: Chemistry
q: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for water is $1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If your automobile radiator holds $1.0 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water, how many grams of ethylene glycol $\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6 \mathrm{O}_2\right)$ must you add to get the freezing point of the solution lowered to $-2.8^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
question: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for water is $1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If your automobile radiator holds $1.0 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water, how many grams of ethylene glycol $\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6 \mathrm{O}_2\right)$ must you add to get the freezing point of the solution lowered to $-2.8^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot \mathrm{m}$
$2.8=1.86 \times \frac{\mathrm{wt}}{62} \times \frac{1000}{1000}$
$\mathrm{Wt}=93 \mathrm{~g}$

subject: Chemistry
q: In a solvent <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of an acid HA dimerizes and the rest dissociates. The van't Hoff factor of the acid is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> (Round off to the nearest integer)
question: In a solvent <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of an acid HA dimerizes and the rest dissociates. The van't Hoff factor of the acid is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> (Round off to the nearest integer)
solution:
Let initial moles of HA is a

2HA→H2 A20.5a       0.25a

 HA⇌H++  A-0.5a   0.5a   0.5a

Now, i= final moles  initial moles =0.25a+0.5a+0.5a0.5a+0.5a

=1.25=125×10-2

αβγδKH5022×10-50.5

(density of water =103 k gm-3 at 298 K ) This table implies that :
question:
Henry's constant (in kbar) for four gases α,β,γ  and δ in water at 298 K is given below :

αβγδKH5022×10-50.5

(density of water =103 k gm-3 at 298 K ) This table implies that :
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> has the highest solubility in water at a given pressure

The pressure of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>55</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> molal solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">δ</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn></math> bar
solution:
(1) From Henery's law P=kHX

Higher the value of kH smaller will be solubility so γ is more soluble.

(2) Though solubility of gas will decrease with increase in temperature but this conclusion can not be drawn from the given table.

(3)For

δ⇒Pδ=KHδ.Xδ

=0.5×103×55.5555.55+100018=250bar.

(4)For γ

pγ=KHγ ×xγ

=2×10-255.555.5+100018=10-2bar

subject: Chemistry
q:
The freezing point of benzene decreases by 0.45°C on adding 0.2 g of acetic acid to 20 g of benzene. If acetic acid associates to form a dimer in benzene, then what is the percentage association of acetic acid in benzene?

Kf for benzene=5.12 K kg mol-1
question:
The freezing point of benzene decreases by 0.45°C on adding 0.2 g of acetic acid to 20 g of benzene. If acetic acid associates to form a dimer in benzene, then what is the percentage association of acetic acid in benzene?

Kf for benzene=5.12 K kg mol-1
solution:
2CH3COOH⇌CH3COOH21-αα2

i=1-α2

ΔTf=i×Kf×m

0.45=i×5.12×0.2×100060×20

i=0.527=1-α2 

⇒α=0.946 or 94.6%

subject: Chemistry
q:
'x' g of molecular oxygen O2 is mixed with 200 g of neon Ne. The total pressure of the nonreactive mixture of O2 and Ne in the cylinder is 25 bar. The partial pressure of Ne is 20 bar at the same temperature and volume. The value of 'x' is

[Given: Molar mass of O2=32 g mol-1. Molar mass of Ne=20 g mol-1]
question:
'x' g of molecular oxygen O2 is mixed with 200 g of neon Ne. The total pressure of the nonreactive mixture of O2 and Ne in the cylinder is 25 bar. The partial pressure of Ne is 20 bar at the same temperature and volume. The value of 'x' is

[Given: Molar mass of O2=32 g mol-1. Molar mass of Ne=20 g mol-1]
solution:
Given

O2+Ne

Xgm 200gm

Ptotal=25 bar; PNe=20

According to Dalton's law of partial pressure.

PO2+PNe=25

PO2=25-20=5 bar

Also, PO2 = XO2PT    Where XO2= mole fraction of oxygen gas.

5=x32x32+20020×25

5x=x+320

4x=320

x=3204=80gm

subject: Chemistry
q: Freezing point of an aqueous solution is $(-0.186)^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Elevation of boiling point of the same solution is $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.512^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, find the increase in boiling point.
question: Freezing point of an aqueous solution is $(-0.186)^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Elevation of boiling point of the same solution is $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.512^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, find the increase in boiling point.
solution: $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}} \times \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}} \times 1000 ; \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}} \times \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}} \times 1000 ; \frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}{-0.186}=\frac{0.512}{1.86}=0.0512^0 \mathrm{C}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following statements are not correct?
A. For water, magnitude of $K_b$ is more than the magnitude of $K_f$.
B. The elevation in boiling point of water when a non-volatile solute is added to it is larger in magnitude than its depression in freezing point.
C. Osmotic pressure measurement is preferred over any other colligative property to determine molar mass of proteins and polymers.
D. The dimerised form of benzoic acid in benzene is $C_6H_5-\underset{O}{\overset{O}{\|}}C-OH \cdots\cdots O=\underset{OH}{\overset{|}{C}}-C_6H_5$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Which of the following statements are not correct?
A. For water, magnitude of $K_b$ is more than the magnitude of $K_f$.
B. The elevation in boiling point of water when a non-volatile solute is added to it is larger in magnitude than its depression in freezing point.
C. Osmotic pressure measurement is preferred over any other colligative property to determine molar mass of proteins and polymers.
D. The dimerised form of benzoic acid in benzene is $C_6H_5-\underset{O}{\overset{O}{\|}}C-OH \cdots\cdots O=\underset{OH}{\overset{|}{C}}-C_6H_5$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
For water, the molal elevation constant $K_b = 0.52 \text{ K kg mol}^{-1}$ and the molal depression constant $K_f = 1.86 \text{ K kg mol}^{-1}$. Thus, $K_b < K_f$. Statement A is incorrect.

For a solution with a given molality $m$ of a non-volatile solute, the elevation in boiling point is $\Delta T_b = K_b m$ and the depression in freezing point is $\Delta T_f = K_f m$. Since $K_b < K_f$, it follows that $\Delta T_b < \Delta T_f$. Statement B is incorrect.

Osmotic pressure measurement is preferred for determining the molar mass of macromolecules such as proteins and polymers because the measurement is done at room temperature and the magnitude of osmotic pressure is significant even for very dilute solutions. Statement C is correct.

In benzene, benzoic acid undergoes dimerization through intermolecular hydrogen bonding, forming a cyclic dimer with two hydrogen bonds. The structure given in statement D represents a linear association with only one hydrogen bond. Statement D is incorrect.

Therefore, statements A, B, and D are incorrect.

Answer: A, B and D only

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of solute A was dissolved in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>62</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of ethanol and freezing point of the solution was found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>155</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The molar mass of solute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [Given: Freezing point of ethanol is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>156</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. Density of ethanol is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Freezing point depression constant of ethanol is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of solute A was dissolved in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>62</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of ethanol and freezing point of the solution was found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>155</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The molar mass of solute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [Given: Freezing point of ethanol is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>156</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. Density of ethanol is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Freezing point depression constant of ethanol is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Given

Mass of C2H5OH=62.5×0.8=50 g 

Mass of solute = 1.80 g

Depression in freezing point ∆Tf = 156.0-155.1 = 0.9 K

We know that the depression in freezing point

ΔTf=Kf×m

0.9=2×1.8×1000Mw×50

Mw=2×1.8×10000.9×50=80

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of acetic acid was contaminated with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ascorbic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>8</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> to lower down its freezing point by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is____ (Nearest integer). [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>; Molar mass of ascorbic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>176</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of acetic acid was contaminated with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ascorbic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>8</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> to lower down its freezing point by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is____ (Nearest integer). [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>; Molar mass of ascorbic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>176</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Given 150 g of solvent CH3COOH

And as solute 10.2 g ascorbic acid =10.2176 = 0.058 moles

ΔTf=x×10-1°C

ΔTf=kf· molality

=3.9×0.058150×1000

=1.5 °C

=15×10-1 °C

So, value of x=15.

subject: Chemistry
q:
At room temperature, a dilute solution of urea is prepared by dissolving 0.60 g of urea in 360 g of water. If the vapour pressure of pure water at this temperature is 35 mm Hg , lowering of vapour pressure will be:
(molar mass of urea =60 g mol-1 )
question:
At room temperature, a dilute solution of urea is prepared by dissolving 0.60 g of urea in 360 g of water. If the vapour pressure of pure water at this temperature is 35 mm Hg , lowering of vapour pressure will be:
(molar mass of urea =60 g mol-1 )
options: <math><mn>0.028</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>

<math><mn>0.027</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>

<math><mn>0.031</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>

<math><mn>0.017</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>
solution:
Relative lowering in vapour Pressure
⇒ Mole of urea nB=0.660=10-2 mole ;

Mole of water nA=36018=20

Po-PsPo=xB=Po-PsPo=nBnn+nB

Here =Po=V.P. of pure solvent

Ps=V.P of solution.

nA+nB≃nA

Po-PsPo=nBnA
lowering of vapour pressure(Po-Ps)=nBnA×Po

Po-Ps=10-220×35

Po-Ps=0.0175  mm of Hg

subject: Chemistry
q: What happens to freezing point of benzene when small quantity of napthalene is added to benzene?
question: What happens to freezing point of benzene when small quantity of napthalene is added to benzene?
solution:
Depression in Freezing point can be calculated as,

∆Tf=i×Kf×m

When a non-volatile component is added to the solvent, its freezing point decreases. Here, Napthalene is a non-volatile substance which decreases the freezing point of benzene.

On addition of naphthalene to benzene there is depression in freezing point of benzene.

Hence, the answer is option D.

subject: Chemistry
q:
A solution containing 2 g of a non-volatile solute in 20 g of water boils at 373.52 K. The molecular mass of the solute is _____ gmol-1. (Nearest integer)

Given, water boils at 373 K,Kb for water =0.52K kg mol-1
question:
A solution containing 2 g of a non-volatile solute in 20 g of water boils at 373.52 K. The molecular mass of the solute is _____ gmol-1. (Nearest integer)

Given, water boils at 373 K,Kb for water =0.52K kg mol-1
solution:
Elevation in boiling point:
⇒ΔTb=373.52-373=0.52

ΔTb=Kb·m

⇒0.52=0.52×2 Molar Mass of Solute ×120×10-3

Molar Mass of solute=100 g/mol

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following could act as apropellant for rockets?
question: Which of the following could act as apropellant for rockets?
solution: Liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen are used as excellent fuel for rockets. $\mathrm{H}_2(l)$ has low mass and high enthalpy of combustion whereas oxygen is a strong supporter of combustion

subject: Chemistry
q:
CO2 gas is bubbled through water during a soft drink manufacturing process at 298 K. If CO2 exerts a partial pressure of 0.835 bar then x m mol of CO2 would dissolve in 0.9 L of water. The value of x is _______. (Nearest integer)

(Henry's law constant for CO2 at 298 K is 1.67×103 bar)
question:
CO2 gas is bubbled through water during a soft drink manufacturing process at 298 K. If CO2 exerts a partial pressure of 0.835 bar then x m mol of CO2 would dissolve in 0.9 L of water. The value of x is _______. (Nearest integer)

(Henry's law constant for CO2 at 298 K is 1.67×103 bar)
solution:
From Henry's law

Pgas=KH·Xgas

0.835=1.67×103×nCO20.9×100018

nCO2=0.025

Millimoles of CO2=0.025×1000=25

subject: Chemistry
q:
What weight of glucose must be dissolved in 100 g of water to lower the vapour pressure by 0.20 mm Hg?
(Assume dilute solution is being formed)
Given: Vapour pressure of pure water is 54.2 mm Hg at room temperature.Molar mass of glucose is 180 g mol-1
question:
What weight of glucose must be dissolved in 100 g of water to lower the vapour pressure by 0.20 mm Hg?
(Assume dilute solution is being formed)
Given: Vapour pressure of pure water is 54.2 mm Hg at room temperature.Molar mass of glucose is 180 g mol-1
solution:
Sol. Using the formula
Po-PsPo=Moles of soluteMoles of solventfor dilute solution

Po=Vapour pressure of pure water

Ps=Vapour pressure of solution
54.2-5454.2=w/18010018w=0.2×100054.2=20054.2=3.69
